I photographed this drake Northern Shoveler at the eastern end of the Auto Tour Route near the Viewing Platform at Sacramento NWR. It is a male still changing from his eclipse-phase into his winter plumage. I have read that drake Northern Shovelers are usually the last duck species to change from their eclipse-phase plumage into their winter / breeding plumage (see second photo for comparison), and this drake (in the first photo) still hasn't fully changed plumage by mid January.

Phylum: Chordata Class: Aves Order: Charadriiformes Morphology: These sandpipers have a beak that is approximately the same length as their heads. Their legs and bill are all black. They're approximately 8 inches long and have a 14 inch wingspan. The color of their feathers can range from whitish-grey to brown and black depending on the time of year. Fun Fact: They have breeding plumage that differs from their non-breeding plumage. Their non-breedin plumage is a white-grey color, and their breeding plumage comes out in the summer and is speckled with brown, black, and white. Their color changes around May, which is about the time that they move north to breeding grounds.

There was a group of around 20 of these foraging in the street. I counted at least 12 males with full blue plumage and 6 others with tan/brown plumage, I assume females, and one upon closer inspection with binoculars looked like a first spring male with mixed blue/brown plumage. With binoculars I could tell they were definitely indigo buntings, having all-blue plumage, a slightly darker blue head, dusty black wing feathers, and no brown wing bars like the blue grosbeak. They would occasionally fly up to a nearby crabapple tree or other trees when startled, and their song seemed to match other indigo bunting recordings.

Three Greater Yellowlegs (Tringa melanoleuca) and a single Willet (Tringa semipalmata), photographed in Harriet Mundy Marsh at Palo Alto Baylands, Palo Alto, CA. All of these birds are returning winter visitors in basic plumage. The Willet on the right can be identified from its pale gray feathers, greenish-yellow legs, rounded body shape, and medium large size (for a sandpiper, anyway). Dowitchers are smaller in size, with smaller heads, more tapered bodies, and darker plumage. Marbled Godwits and Long-billed Curlews are notieably larger than Willets, with plumage that is speckled with black and white markings on a buffy background color. The three Greater Yellowlegs on the left are similar in size to the Willet, but with bright, yellow-orange legs and a less rotund body. The tips of their primaries are black, while the rest of their plumage is dark gray with white speckles. They are very similar to Lesser Yellowlegs, which is also a possibility in the San Francisco Bay Area area during the winter months. However, the Lesser Yellowlegs is only about 60% of the size of a Willet, so none of these birds could be one. The only Yellowlegs whose bill is not tucked under a wing further confirms that these birds are all Greater Yellowlegs. Note that the slight upcurve at the tip of its bill; in Lesser Yellowlegs, the bill is straight. Also note the two-tone coloration of its bill (grayish at the base, black at the tip), which appears once the bird has molted into basic plumage. The bill of a Lesser Yellowlegs is always black, regardless of plumage. Since the other two birds are the same size as this one, they are all Greater Yellowlegs.

With Paul Gray, Greg Schrott, and James Tucker; Survey 1 of 5 of Lake Okeechobee (Okeechobee County) mudflats by shorebirds and larids during drought conditions, by Audubon airboat. The lake had attained historic low levels, exposing dozens of square miles of mufdlats, which were barren during earlier surveys but vegetated up to 2 meters tall during later surveys. Owing to little previous work in Lake Okeechobee during drought conditions, as well as little work in Okeechobee County, we documented 10 "firsts" for Okeechobee County and 6 "firsts" (and 2 additional breeding "firsts") for Lake Okeechobee. We left at 1715 after 8 hours and 5 minutes, and headed back to Avon Park. We published a paper presenting the results of our five surveys, in Florida Field Naturalist 37(2): 33-44, 2009. https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2140&context=ffn Notable species (ph. refers to a photograph taken by BP): Lesser Scaup: one presumed hunting cripple on 8 Jun (ph.). American White Pelican: 64 on 13 Apr (ph.) and 10 on 8 Jun. Roseate Spoonbill: 65 (no adults in alternate plumage) on 27 Jul (ph.). Semipalmated Plover: observed on 4 of the 5 surveys (none 27 Jul, when our airboat experienced mechanical issues), with 251 on 8 May (ph.), providing the first for Okeechobee County. Black-necked Stilt: common breeding resident, with >100 on each trip (ph.). Western Willet: singles on 8 May (ph.) and 18 Aug (ph.) furnished the firsts for Okeechobee County. Red Knot: singles on 13 Apr and 8 May furnished the firsts for Okeechobee County. Sanderling: 2 in basic plumage on 8 May furnished the first for Okeechobee County and Lake Okeechobee. Western Sandpiper: as many as 10 on 4 surveys furnished the firsts for Okeechobee County. White-rumped Sandpiper: 3 on 8 May and 3 on 8 Jun furnished the firsts for Okeechobee County. Pectoral Sandpiper: 1 on 13 Apr and 16 on 19 Aug furnished the first for Okeechobee County. Dunlin: a flock of 17 (some in alternate plumage) on 13 Apr (ph.) and 10 (mostly in alternate plumage) on 8 May (ph.) furnished the first for Lake Okeechobee. Stilt Sandpiper: 445 on 13 Apr (many in alternate plumage). Buff-breated Sandpiper: 6 on 13 Apr (including 1 in courtship display; ph.) furnished the first for Okeechobee County and Lake Okeechobee. Additionally, the courtship display had never been observed in Florida previously! Long-billed Dowitcher: 1,180 in alternate plumage on 13 Apr furnished the first for Lake Okeechobee! Red-necked Phalarope: 1 female on 8 May furnished the first for Okeechobee County and Lake Okeechobee. American Herring Gull: 73 on 13 Apr (ph.). Western Lesser Black-backed Gull: 2 (adult and third-cycle) on 13 Apr (ph.) furnished the first for Okeechobee County and Lake Okeechobee. Gull-billed Tern: 2 on 13 Apr (ph.) and singles on 8 May and 19 Aug, were in basic plumage. Least Tern: a tiny island off the mouth of the Kissimee River furnished the first breeding for Lake Okeechobee: 13 adults on 13 Apr (ph.), 10 adults and one nest with 2 eggs on 8 May (ph.), and 16 adults, one juvenile, and one large nestling on 8 Jun (ph.). Caspian Tern: 10, with 7 in alternate plumage, on 13 Apr (ph.). Black Skimmer: a large non-breeding flock winters at Jaycee Park; we estimated 1,000 on 13 Apr (ph.), with 189 to 8 Jun (ph.). Gray Kingbird: a family of 5 on 27 Jul, and 2 there on 27 Jul furnished the first breeding for Lake Okeechobee. Daily totals (shorebirds): 13 Apr: 19,573 8 May: 7,794 8 Jun: 442 27 Jul (truncated visit): 665 19 Aug: 1,485
SOURCE: R.C.MILLER, AND MRS. R.C.MILLER.; HOW AGED: PLUMAGE. SKULL: NOT PNEUMATIZED. COMMENT: SKULL DRAWN.

3 adult loons to the West of the island. 1 loon apparently dozing (?) for a bit while other 2 loons drifted nearby. Chicks were not seen at this time. 1 of the loons was noticeably smaller than the Somes Pond territorial pair, but in breeding plumage. Photographs taken by Ray Yaeger show somewhat scraggly breeding plumage on the intruding bird--suspect maybe a young loon 1st year in adult summer plumage (?). 1 of SP adults then splash-dived toward the smaller loon, who subsequently took off circling the pond once before leaving to the Southeast. Somes pond pair moved East and rejoined chicks along the North shore of the pond. Both adults dove frequently, resurfacing as far as 100’ feet from the chicks on occasion. 1 chick fed small fish by adult. Occasional soft hoot calls made by adults.

Large body and wings. Long legs and neck. Base of neck is thick where it meats the body and tapers in towards the head. Red plumage on head around and above eye. White plumage underneath eye. A mix of grey blue, white and light brown plumage on body. Coloration becoming a more consistent light blue-grey on neck and head. Small yellow eyes. Long pointed sharp beak, dark grey color. The large body, long neck and legs lead me to believe that this is a member of Gruiformes. The specific coloration of the plumage and that around the eyes lead me to think that these are Sandhill Cranes.
Molt: S1,4,5=juvenal plumage; Misc: RBL-81-2, small fat patches inside abdomen, no fat on skin; Plumage:  alternate 2

A Mute Swan (Cygnus olor), photographed at Shollenberger Park in Petaluma, CA. The all-white plumage indicates taht this bird is an adult, and the pinkish-orange bill is distinctive for Mute Swan. Tundra Swan (Cygnus columbianus) also has all-white plumage in adulthood, but it is a smaller, lighter-looking bird with an all-black bill. Trumpeter Swan (Cygnus buccinator) is also a possibility as a rare vagrant to California skies; like the C. columbianus, it has all-white plumage in adulthood, with an all-black bill.

Large mergansers with richer colors and higher contrasts than RBME and a clean demarcation between head and neck, a cleanly defined white area at the chin. It took a while to review these birds as they appeared different from each other and they appear to be 2021 (SY) birds. Diving in, it turns out they are the most difficult of our three mergansers to age, and to sex when they are young. They both had yellow, not brown eyes (e.g. eye color of more mature birds) https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/305929581 https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/377271211 The one in the bay had more consistently grey sides (and when it flew a narrow black line showed at the tips of all the greater coverts, including the inner ones), whereas the one in QMR had a lot of white marbling in the grey on its sides, just in front of the bend of the wing (perhaps a male? in study), and it also had a more richly colored bill. It had two grey feathers sitting over the white speculum on either wing, and a couple of dark grey feathers coming in midway on the sides at the water line. Both had a dark stripe on the culmen. Just to double check I went back and found the one on the bay, snoozing with RBMG (37.806848, -122.457217) then rode over to QMR and three minutes later the marbled one was foraging in the creek that leads south. More research netted an article called "Mergansers: ID, ageing and sexing" by Tony Leukering. He writes, "... >500 photos of Common Mergansers in the eBird/Macaulay Library photo archive2 (Sullivan 2009) and at Flickr3 and found <8 photos of Common Mergansers that I could determine (via plumage, eye color) were immature males, even when restricting my search to November through May. Conversely, there are numerous photos of immature male Red-breasted Mergansers discoverable with similar searches. I suspect that many or most immature male Common Mergansers make the transition to adult-like plumage quite late, if at all. One of the causes of this relative lack of photographic material of immature male Commons in plumage transition may be due to its relatively earlier spring migration (personal observation, eBird 20124), which could mean that the molt takes place mostly on or near the breeding grounds, where they are less accessible to most photographers. ... adult males attain a variably female-like plumage May-July and molt out of that plumage August-October (Pyle 2008, Dugger et al. 2009, Craik et al. 2015, Pearce et al. 2015)" So these aren't adult males in alternate (dull) plumage (it's flipped around in ducks, haha ... a few months ago you'd likely need to see an open wing to sex adults) as we've seen those fly by recently in bright plumage, so, timing. M.m. americanus by the feathering at the base of the upper mandible looks like a "2" (on the nominate and orientalis it looks like a 'Z'), the nostrils are found midway on the bill, and lack of a hook at the tip. Compare the eurostyle: https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/404746081

The Pelagic Cormorant are commonly seen roosting on the pilings at the Anacortes Ferry Terminal. I identified the species by the body shape, iridescent black feathers, and the white patches and crests (breeding plumage). I observed them nesting inside the pilings and on top, some of them outstretching their wings to dry their feathers (cormorant plumage is not waterproof).

The darters, anhingas, or snakebirds are mainly tropical waterbirds in the family Anhingidae. Anhingidae are large birds with sexually dimorphic plumage. They measure about 80 to 100 cm (2.6 to 3.3 ft) in length, with a wingspan around 120 cm (3.9 ft), and weigh some 1,050 to 1,350 grams (37 to 48 oz). The males have black and dark-brown plumage, a short erectile crest on the nape and a larger bill than the female. The females have much paler plumage, especially on the neck and underparts, and are a bit larger overall. Both have grey stippling on long scapulars and upper wing coverts. The sharply pointed bill has serrated edges, a desmognathous palate and no external nostrils. The darters have completely webbed feet, and their legs are short and set far back on the body.

Long-tailed Duck • Trophic level: mainly secondary consumer • Description: A winter visitor from the Arctic. Females are unassuming in plumage and easy to mistake for other winter ducks from a distance, but males are quite striking with the signature cowlick-like tail, sleek black and white plumage, pink bill stripe, and headphone-like dark plumage beneath the eyes. Males also make a distinctly bizarre honking call when courting females. • Life Cycle: 6 to 8 eggs incubated by the female for a month. Young, like many ducks, feed themselves but follow their mother around. Breed in the Arctic and winter along the coasts on either side of the northern portion of North America. • Stage: Adult, based on time and appearance • Sex: Both males and females are pictured. • Habitat: The ocean or large lakes, often far from the shore. During breeding, tundra pools and lakes. • Predator: Arctic Fox • Prey: Mollusks like the Atlantic Ribbed Mussel • Competitor: Common Eider

Hello, We are Lance and Jill Morrow and have been working on aberrant plumages in birds. We have recently published on dilute plumage in kestrels (Texas Ornithological Society) and melanistic, one aberrant dark and two aberrant pale Coopers Hawks (J. Raptor Research). In the early 1970's a pale/dilute plumaged White-Throated Sparrow (Zonotrichia albicollis) was collected in Northern Virginia by Lance Morrow (Morrow, L.). This study skin was part of Walter Balmer's (of Northern Virginia Community College) extensive collection which, we hope, is now under your care. Could you locate this specimen and possibly take photos for publication? Small lone sandpiper that I just wasn't sure about. Seemed more upright and long-legged than a Least. Very long toes. Update: After posting images and a video on OBOL and reading a few IDs of Least Sandpiper (see below for a detailed explanation from ornithologist, Wayne Hoffman), I have edited the ID. "This bird has several features that resemble a Long-toed Stint, but two features of the head and face are more typical of Least Sandpiper. 1. Most Long-toed Stints have some yellow on the base of the lower mandible, and most Least Sandpipers do not. This bird appears to have an all-black bill. 2. The face pattern of Long-toed Stints typically has the white supercilium hooking down in front of the eye, and no dark line across the lores, and the dark of the crown continues forward to meet the top of the bill. Leasts usually have the supercilia meet at the top of the bill, separating it from the crown, as well as a dark mark across the lores as in this bird. The frustrating thing about trying to identify a Long-toed Stint is that they have several features that generally differ from Leasts, but there is some overlap in nearly every one. The only apparent exception is voice. As the name indicates, Long-toed Stints have longer toes. However, there seems to be a lot of variation in Least Sandpiper toe lengths, and some birds with toes approaching LT Stint length have some of the plumage characters of Leasts. Long-toed Stints tend to stand more erect, and look longer-legged, but some birds. like yours combine this habitat with plumage more like Leasts. I get the impression that Least sandpipers vary more in plumage and body proportions than most other sandpipers. I suspect that Long-toed Stints also vary in some of the plumage features we depend on to separate them, but currently in North America, a Long-toed Stint has to be recorded vocalizing, or to be typical in ALL the features people look for, to be conclusively identified. I have an image stuck in my brain of a birder in, say Thailand, picking through flocks of Long-toed Stints looking for a vagrant Least, and rejecting candidate after candidate for not being quite right: "That looks like a Least but the bill looks a little paler at the base...: "That looks like a Least but the line across the lores is not very distinct..." etc. In reality, I suspect that there may be as many stray Long-toeds to North America as Red-necked Stints or Little Stints, but most overlap enough in plumage with Leasts that we do not recognize them."

It was around 5 p.m. with temperatures around the 70’s degrees Fahrenheit when I encounter this majestic bird of prey. The osprey looked healthy and strong. The bird seemed calm, almost like it was resting, and also a little intrigued due to my presence near its nest. In other words, this bird of prey appears to like to keep a safe distance from humans. The Osprey is also known as the sea hawk, river hawk, and fish hawk. They can weight up to 1.5 to 2.0 kg with a wingspan of 1.6. A brown above and white below plumage with a brown stripe through their eye is what makes their plumage unique from other raptors. Class: Aves- tetrapods with feathers. Order: Accipitriformes- diurnal raptors. (Therefore can’t be a Strigiformes.) Family: Pandionidae. No Chiselshaped bill, so it can’t be a Piciformes. Middle size bird, so not a Falconiformes. Accipitriformes have powerful raptorial talons and bills and large. Some of them are scavengers, and others are active hunters. Most of the time the females are bigger than the males making them sexually dimorphic. Their plumage is built for ambush attacks, meaning that feathers match their habitats.
1) Shorter yellow bill compared to the Great egret 2) Short black legs 3) Forages along side livestock 4) Non-breeding plumage includes all white plumage

The common starling, also known simply as the starling in Great Britain and Ireland, and as European starling in North America, is a medium-sized passerine bird in the starling family, Sturnidae. It is about 20 cm long and has glossy black plumage with a metallic sheen, which is speckled with white at some times of the year. The legs are pink and the bill is black in winter and yellow in summer; young birds have browner plumage than the adults.

These pelicans had their coated winter plumage, and some were juveniles. None of them had the colorful white and red plumage from summer. Two large groups of these pelicans flew over Elkhorn Slough, this being one of them. They took a "V" formation, even though it wasn't as synchronized as the "V"s that geese would make. They lived in the Salt Marsh.

These are some geese that have been moving around the Umpqua Valleys for a few months that at first glance look a lot like Snow Geese, but they do not have black primaries, don't have the black "smile" and they do have small scattered black spots/splotches in their plumage. One or more also have some grayish in the plumage, like immature Snow Geese. I don't know exactly how to classify them. They are not as large as a typical "barnyard" goose.

I found this gorgeous Black-crowned Night-Heron at Paynes Prairie around 5 pm the weather was around the 70’s degrees Fahrenheit. This magnificent bird seems to be shy compared to the other birds that I was able to encounter that day because as soon as I approach this bird it will fly to a different spot looking for safety from all the flashes from the cameras of the bird fanatics. Otherwise, this bird seems to be in good shape, and its plumage was a clear indication of its health. This Black-crowned Night-Heron weights around 800 g with a length of 25 inches long. They have red eyes, and their plumage tends to be light-gray with a black back and black crown. Juveniles are brown with large white spots on the wings and blurry streaks on the underparts. Also, adults have all black bills, and juveniles have a mix of yellow and black. Class: Aves – tetrapod with plumage. Order: Pelecaniformes- long-legged and long-necked wading birds. So not Gruiformes.) Family: Ardeidae. No stout bills; therefore, not a Ciconiiformes. No totipalmate feed, so not Suliformes. Pelecaniformes are widely distribute composed of long-legged and long-necked wading birds with long, pointed beaks. Herons are known for being ambush predators that use their beak to stab their prey with their pointed bills.

The common starling (Sturnus vulgaris), also known simply as the starling in Great Britain and Ireland, and as European starling in North America, is a medium-sized passerine bird in the starling family, Sturnidae. It is about 20 cm (8 in) long and has glossy black plumage with a metallic sheen, which is speckled with white at some times of the year. The legs are pink and the bill is black in winter and yellow in summer; young birds have browner plumage than the adults. Its gift for mimicry has been noted in literature including the Mabinogion and the works of Pliny the Elder and William Shakespeare.

ID suggested by iNaturalist, in addition to this explanation from ai: Based on the image from Boca Chita Key in South Florida, the bird is a white wading bird with a relatively long neck, legs, and a pointed bill, standing near the water's edge. Given these features and the coastal Florida location, it is most likely a juvenile Little Blue Heron in its white plumage phase, or possibly a Snowy Egret. Here's why: Juvenile Little Blue Heron (Egretta caerulea): Young Little Blue Herons are entirely white and have a pale bill (often bluish or greenish-gray with a darker tip) and duller legs than adult Snowy Egrets. As they mature, they gradually acquire their characteristic blue-gray plumage. The bill in your photo appears light. Snowy Egret (Egretta thula): These are entirely white adults with black legs and distinctive yellow feet ("golden slippers"), and a slender black bill. If the bird in the photo has black legs and yellow feet, it would be a Snowy Egret. However, the leg color isn't clear enough to definitively see the yellow feet, and the bill appears lighter than a typical Snowy Egret's black bill. Given the bill's apparent pale color and the overall form, a juvenile Little Blue Heron is a very strong possibility, as they are commonly seen in such coastal habitats in Florida.

Hybrid Northern X Tropical Parula. Would not let me proceed without selecting one specific species so chose to enter as "Northern" Parula based on the fact that this species breeds in this location. .  .  Appears to be the same bird I photographed in September of 2013. Same general area as the bird I had previously observed. Male, singing on territory. .  .  Lacked dark necklace of Northern but with traces of darker coloration in same area. More extensive yellow, extending down the belly. Presence of incomplete, lower eye-arc (no upper eye-arc) and blotchy, well-defined orange coloration on the breast seem to suggest a hybrid Northern X Tropical Parula. .  .  Knocks against pure Tropical lineage are the presence of a lower eye-arc, lack of a smooth transition of yellow-orange coloration on the breast and belly (there's a well-defined blotch of orange that doesn't "fade" into the yellow), weak, black mask and darker coloration near the throat, which hints at a breast band. .  .  Several years ago, there was a record of TRPA from nearby Hamilton Pool. It is possible that this bird could be the offspring if that bird nested.; animal seen:yes; playback used:yes

Most of our house finches are display bright scarlet coloration, but this one is displaying very distinctive yellow coloration. I take it that the diet of house finches plays a role in their coloration and that this specimen has been eating something that has led to this coloration. "But a careful look at male House Finches at the feeder shows that, while most males show red feathering, some are decidedly more orange – and some even yellow. It turns out that House Finches acquire their coloration from pigments in the plant foods they eat." https://www.audubon.org/news/why-male-house-finches-can-be-different-colors.

Natural song from a male Haemorhous finch. Given the unusual song type and proximity to previous recording, likely the same individual as XC472344. The chest streaking and extent of coloration suggest Purple Finch (Haemorhous pupureus), however, the coloration was orange-yellow rather than raspberry red. The Great Blue Heron is named after the overall coloration of its blue and gray appearance. Over the eye lies a black stripe. Due to observing the bird on land, I went to external sources to describe the coloration of the upper side of their wings. According to the Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Great Blue Herons have dark feathers in the back of their wings and an off-white, pale color on their forewing (“Great Blue Heron Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology”). Alongside the unique coloration of the North American bird, its shape is unique as well. Starting at the head, the bill of the bird is of orange coloration. A defining characteristic of the taxonomic group, specifically the family, Ardeidae Leach in which herons belong to, is the daggerlike beak that allows them to prey on fish by grabbing them in the water easily. Its neck is long and S-shaped to allow for swift predation of fish in water. The wings of the bird are rounded. The Great Blue Heron has long skinny legs, contributing to its approximately 4.5-foot-tall height. The reproductive structure is not present in the picture taken of the Great Blue Heron. According to the Canadian Wildlife Federation, where Great Blue Herons reside is different from where they mate (“Great Blue Heron”). The birds are located in freshwater and saltwater coastal environments (“Great Blue Heron”). They also scavenge for food in grass fields. To avoid being preyed upon, they nest in swamps or islands (“Great Blue Heron”). The Great Blue Heron in the picture was found at Lettuce Lake Park in Tampa, Florida. According to National Geographic, the geographic distribution of Great Blue Herons is common across most of North America ranging to Alaska in the summer (“The Great Blue Heron”). During the winter, the distribution varies from islands such as the Galapagos Islands and the Caribbean and throughout Florida, South America, and Mexico (“The Great Blue Heron”). Works Cited: “Great Blue Heron.” Hinterland Who's Who - Boreal Forest, Canadian Wildlife Federation, www.hww.ca/en/wildlife/birds/great-blue-heron.html. “Great Blue Heron Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology.”, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Cornell Lab of Ornithology, www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Great_Blue_Heron/id. “The Great Blue Heron.” Great Blue Heron National Geographic, National Geographic, 24 Sept. 2018, www.nationalgeographic.com/animals/birds/g/great-blue-heron/. With regards to the identification of the Great Blue Heron depicted in the observation, I determined the species by first utilizing the suggestions option on iNaturalist. After the app suggested it was a Great Blue Heron, I doubled checked by searching the internet for images of the bird. There I found a website produced by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology that displayed pictures and close-up illustrations of the heron. From analyzing the image, I noticed the similar blue-gray coloration of the body of the bird from my observation and the image from Cornell that also depicted the same coloration. Other distinguishing characteristics such as the dagger-like bill and the black stripe behind the eye of the image on the website solidified the determination of the species of bird being a Great Blue Heron.

White ibises have a distinctive long, curved bill and a slender, wading bird shape. The ones in the photos are barely over a foot tall in height. Their coloration is primarily white with a pinkish/orangish hue to their legs and face. One defining characteristic of Florida white ibises that places them within the taxonomic group of Eudocimus is their overall plumage coloration. Species within the Eudocimus genus are characterized by their bright white plumage, contrasting with their pink/orangish face, legs, and bill. This distinctive coloration is a hallmark of the Eudocimus group, which also includes the closely related scarlet ibis. I found this bird in the grass field of Mirror Lake City Park, St Petersburg, Florida. The known geographic distribution of white ibises are limited to areas in the southeastern United States (notably Florida), Central and South America, and the Caribbean. They are oftentimes found in wetlands, marshes, and coastal regions of these areas. References https://sta.uwi.edu/fst/lifesciences/sites/default/files/lifesciences/documents/ogatt/Eudocimus_albus%20-%20American%20White%20Ibis.pdf https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Eudocimus_albus/

This organism has a tall neck and legs that significantly contribute to the height of this organism. This individual possesses gray feathers. The legs of this organism are black, which is an important distinction of coloration in this individual. When compared to the rest of the body, this organism has a less gray and more white colored face. There is a red coloration that covers the head of this organism. This individual has a straight, black beak. A defining characteristic of this species is the coloration of its body. Most Sandhill Cranes have a gray body and pale cheeks. Also present is the "red skin on the crown" and that their legs are black. All of these features are present in this observation, which make this organism indicative of a Sandhill Crane (https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Sandhill_Crane/id). These birds are largely found in North America, and most go south to Mexico and Cuba, or west to Siberia. In the winters, these birds migrate down to "Florida, Texas, Utah, Mexico, and California" (https://www.nationalgeographic.com/animals/birds/s/sandhill-crane/). A typical habitat of this organism is "marshes, bogs, wet meadows, prairies, burned-over aspen stands, and other moist habitats" (https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Sandhill_Crane/lifehistory#habitat).

Native. The wild turkey (Meleagris gallopavo) is an upland game bird native to North America, one of two extant species of turkey. Fully-grown wild turkeys have long, reddish-yellow to grayish-green legs. Each foot has three front toes, with a shorter, rear-facing toe. Females have feathers that are duller overall, in shades of brown and gray. Parasites can dull the coloration of both sexes; in males, vivid coloration may serve as a signal of health. The primary wing feathers have white bars. Turkeys have approximately 5,000 to 6,000 feathers.

While in Boston, I was able to observe two birds in a copulation event with my friend. I watched as what I assume to be the male Sparrow, which I identify as a House Sparrow due to its slightly ruddy coloration, and common nature in Boston, mount the female several times. The coloration of the male's neck was a deep black, due likely to his substantial diet during mating season, and it appeared the female sparrow saw him fit enough to take as a mate. What surprised me most was how close we were able to get to this encounter, as I assumed these birds would take more care to mate in a safe environment, rather than just a few arms' lengths from observing humans.

The Black Skimmer is approximately 4-5 inches tall and is covered in black feathers on the top of the body and white feathers on the inferior side of the body. The beak is red-orange coloration closest to the face and fades into a black coloration. The shape of the beak is distinct in which the bottom protrudes past the top. The long lower mandible is an adaptation to “skim” the water and feel for fish which allows them to feed both during the night and the day (Cornell Lab of Ornithology). These birds are found often on sandy beaches as seen here, but can also be found on sparsely vegetated gravel or shell bars, or in salt marshes (Cornell Lab of Ornithology). References Black Skimmer Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology. (n.d.). Retrieved from https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Black_Skimmer/id

A Leucistic Canada goose is a Canada goose (Branta canadensis) with a genetic condition called leucism, which causes partial loss of pigmentation in feathers, skin, or scales. Unlike albinism, which is a complete lack of melanin (and results in pink eyes, legs, and bill), leucism produces patchy or overall pale coloration while the eyes, legs, and bill remain their normal dark color. Key Features • Coloration: Can appear pale gray, creamy white, or mottled with white patches mixed into the usual black and brown plumage. • Head/neck: Some still retain the characteristic black neck and white cheek patch, while others may look much lighter overall. • Eyes/bill/legs: Stay dark, distinguishing leucism from albinism. • Health: Leucism doesn’t typically affect the goose’s health, though lighter birds may stand out more to predators. Rarity Leucistic Canada geese are rare, but they are occasionally spotted within flocks. Birders often mistake them for albinos or even for domestic goose hybrids because of the unusual plumage.

I photographed this woodpecker in Connecticut, which displays a vibrant and consistent yellow coloration across its body, including the chest, belly, and even some wing feathers. This coloration is significantly different from typical plumage seen in local woodpeckers like Hairy Woodpecker or Yellow-bellied Sapsucker. • The bird is larger than a Red-bellied Woodpecker, with clear black-and-white patterning on the wings. • It lacks the typical red patch of a male sapsucker but shows a clean yellow wash. • This yellow hue is far brighter and more extensive than typical staining from fungi or sap. • I suspect this may be a case of xanthochroism (a genetic mutation causing yellow pigmentation). I would appreciate any insights or confirmation of this possible mutation. Has anyone seen similar cases in this region?

Observed here is a Buteo which is a relatively large bird, categorized as a deuterostome. This bird has a robust body with broad wings. In the picture shown here its length is about 12-14 inches from head to tail, however, its wingspan can expand from 26-63 inches. This bird has a light and dark brown coloration, ranging from light to darker from head to tail and wings. The wings are spotted, and the underside of the bird is a much lighter color when wings are expanded. The bird also has a yellow coloration on the top of its short fat beak. While these birds nest in larger trees, they often hunt in open country and forest edges which is most likely what this particular bird was doing as it was found in an open public place. They occur primarily in lowland and foothill habitats. Buteos reproduce sexually in stable pairs in which they mate for life. A defining characteristic of the Buteo is its broad brown chest and white belly. https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Buteo_brachyurus/

An individual *Charadrius semipalmatus* walking along the shoreline near some feeding *Calidris alba rubica* and *Calidris alpina*. Horribly AI upscaled photos as the cover and photos at the end :(. This is an image of a juvenile little blue heron. Juveniles are typically white, long-legged wading birds with a long, S-shaped neck and a two-toned dagger like bill. They range from 22.1-29.1 inches in length, weigh between 10.4-14.5 oz, and have a wingspan of 39.4-41.3 inches. Their long legs and long curved neck are distinctive features that help this species catch resources. However, to distinguish the type of heron it is one can examine the beak to see if it’s two toned or not. Since the picture of the organism, I took had a two-toned beak, s-shaped neck, and leg coloration, it was identified as a little blue heron. They are usually found in marine and freshwater environments near marshes. This species range distribution is across South America, North American, Africa, Asia, Europe, and Australia Reference: https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Little_Blue_Heron/id

Found on 5/23/2021 at 11:47 AM in the Northdale area. Visual Description: The organism is about 4 inches in length and has a black and white pattern throughout the head and back and brown coloration on the wings. The organism is a bird, so it is Habitat: The Carolina chickadee has made its home in a tree, above a small pond. The area is humid and subtropical. They typically live at lower elevations. Identifying Information: The Carolina chickadees are distinguishable from other species because of the prevalent black cap that extends from their head to the nape. They have a white patch that extends from the beak to the back. These species have nearly identical coloration for males and females, so the only way to tell is through behavioral observation. Reference: I got this information from Florida's Birds: A Field Guide and Reference, written by the University of Florida.
